senses.definition: A member of the Aukan (Ndyuka) people of eastern Suriname.
examples: The guide was an Aucaner who knew every bend of the river.
Aucaners have preserved many elements of their traditional culture.

senses.definition: Relating to the Aucaner (Aukan) people, their language, or their culture.
examples: She is writing a thesis on Aucanian religious practices.
The museum has a small display of Aucanian art.

senses.definition: A person who catches birds, especially for food or for sale.
examples: In Roman times, an auceps might supply birds for the tables of the rich.
The old manuscript mentions an auceps living on the edge of the wetlands.

senses.definition: Plural of auceps: people who catch birds, especially for food or for sale.
examples: The aucepses gathered in the market to sell their birds.
In the illustration, several aucepses are shown working with nets.

senses.definition: An old scientific name for a group of South American camel-like animals such as llamas and guanacos.
examples: The genus Auchenia is mentioned in older zoological texts.
Farmers once bred animals listed under Auchenia for their wool.

senses.definition: The neck or nape, especially in animals, in older anatomical writing.
examples: The injury was located at the base of the horse's auchenium.
An old diagram labels the bird's auchenium rather than simply its neck.

senses.definition: A small piece or scrap of land, especially in Scottish usage.
examples: He owned only an auchlet beside the river.
Each farmer worked a narrow auchlet along the hillside.

senses.definition: Scottish or northern English form of ought, meaning should.
examples: Ye aucht tae help yer neighbours when they are in need.
He said I aucht no tae worry aboot it.

senses.definition: The largest city in New Zealand, on the North Island.
examples: They are planning a holiday in Auckland next summer.
Auckland is known for its harbours and multicultural population.

senses.definition: A sum of money added to a price, fee, or payment, especially in historical or legal use.
examples: An auctary was demanded before the deed was sealed.
The contract mentions an auctary to be paid on completion.

senses.definition: A public sale where things are sold to the person who offers the most money.
examples: They bought the painting at an online auction.
The house will be sold by auction next month.

senses.definition: To sell something in a public sale to the person who offers the most money.
examples: The company will auction its old equipment.
They decided to auction the furniture to raise money.

senses.definition: Relating to an auction or auctions.
examples: The firm specializes in auctionary services.
He wrote about the auctionary process in the art market.

senses.definition: Sold in an auction.
examples: The painting was auctioned for a record price.
Several rare books were auctioned yesterday.

senses.definition: A person whose job is to run an auction and accept bids.
examples: The auctioneer spoke quickly as the bids increased.
She trained as an auctioneer after working in an art gallery.

senses.definition: Acted as an auctioneer, especially for a particular sale.
examples: He auctioneered the estate sale over the weekend.
She has auctioneered many important art collections.

senses.definition: The job or business of running auctions.
examples: He has thirty years of experience in auctioneering.
Auctioneering requires both product knowledge and strong speaking skills.

senses.definition: Plural of auctioneer: people whose job is to run auctions and accept bids.
examples: The company employs several experienced auctioneers.
Auctioneers must be able to think and speak quickly.

senses.definition: Selling something by auction.
examples: They are auctioning old office furniture this week.
The charity is auctioning signed sports shirts online.

senses.definition: Plural of auction: public sales where things are sold to the person who offers the most money.
examples: He often buys antiques at local auctions.
Online auctions have become very popular.

senses.definition: Sells something by auction.
examples: The company auctions surplus stock every year.
He auctions rare books for collectors.

senses.definition: An author or originator, especially in historical or Latin-based usage.
examples: The medieval auctor is known only from initials in the margin.
Each passage is carefully attributed to its original auctor.

senses.definition: Relating to an author or their role in a text.
examples: The novel is full of auctorial comments to the reader.
She analysed the auctorial voice in Victorian fiction.

senses.definition: To authorize or give formal approval to something, in very rare or historical use.
examples: The council did not auctorizate such a payment.
Only the king could auctorizate the treaty.

senses.definition: Plural of auctor: authors or originators, especially in historical or Latin-based writing.
examples: The text draws on several classical auctors.
These arguments were accepted by later auctors.

senses.definition: An evergreen shrub from eastern Asia with glossy leaves and red berries, often grown as a garden plant.
examples: An aucuba grew in the shaded corner of the garden.
They planted aucuba along the wall for year-round colour.

senses.definition: Plural of aucuba: evergreen shrubs with glossy leaves and red berries, often grown as garden plants.
examples: A row of aucubas lined the driveway.
The shady border was filled with aucubas and ferns.

senses.definition: To hunt or catch birds, especially as a sport or occupation, in rare or literary use.
examples: He would aucupate from dawn until noon in the marshes.
Noblemen once aucupated with nets and trained birds.

senses.definition: Abbreviation for Australian dollar, the official currency of Australia.
examples: The price is listed in AUD, not in US dollars.
He converted his savings from AUD to euros.

senses.definition: Abbreviation for auditory, relating to hearing, used in medical and technical writing.
examples: The doctor noted reduced function in the left AUD nerve.
These data come from the AUD cortex of the brain.

senses.definition: Boldness or daring behaviour, especially in a stylish or artistic way.
examples: The designer's audace shocked the traditional fashion world.
There is a certain audace in her decision to speak so openly.

senses.definition: Showing a willingness to take bold risks that may surprise or shock people.
examples: Her audacious plan saved the failing company.
He made an audacious move to challenge the leader.

senses.definition: Rude or disrespectful in a confident way.
examples: It was audacious of him to question her honesty.
The journalist asked an audacious question at the press conference.

senses.definition: In a way that shows boldness and a willingness to take risks.
examples: The team audaciously expanded into three new markets.
She audaciously challenged the traditional rules.

senses.definition: The quality of being very bold and willing to take risks.
examples: Investors admired the audaciousness of the project.
Her audaciousness changed the direction of the debate.

senses.definition: Plural of audaciousness: different kinds or examples of bold, risk-taking behaviour.
examples: The novel celebrates the various audaciousnesses of its heroes.
Their different audaciousnesses helped the start-up to grow quickly.

senses.definition: Plural of audacity: acts or examples of bold or shocking behaviour.
examples: His many audacities made him famous and feared.
The film shows the political audacities of that period.

senses.definition: The confidence to take bold risks that may seem surprising or shocking.
examples: She had the audacity to start her own company at eighteen.
It took real audacity to speak out against the plan.

senses.definition: Rude or shocking behaviour that shows a lack of respect.
examples: I could not believe his audacity in criticising her in public.
She had the audacity to ignore the invitation completely.

senses.definition: A wild sheep from North Africa with long hair on its chest and legs, sometimes hunted for sport.
examples: The park is home to a small herd of audad.
He photographed an audad standing on the rocky slope.

senses.definition: Plural of audad: wild sheep from North Africa with long hair on the chest and legs.
examples: Several audads grazed near the cliff.
Audads have been introduced into some parts of North America.

senses.definition: A member of a small early Christian sect considered heretical, mentioned only in historical texts.
examples: The council officially condemned the Audaean as a heretic.
Little is known about what the Audaeans actually believed.

senses.definition: Relating to the Audaeans or their religious beliefs.
examples: The bishop wrote a letter against Audaean teachings.
These Audaean doctrines were later forgotten.

senses.definition: Relating to hearing or to the sense of hearing.
examples: The film creates a powerful audial atmosphere.
The study compared visual and audial memory.

senses.definition: A member of the Audaean religious sect in early Christianity, used only in historical writing.
examples: The council debated what to do with the Audians.
Audians were criticised for their unusual festivals.

senses.definition: Relating to the Audians or their beliefs.
examples: Audian traditions differed from those of other churches.
The text explains several Audian doctrines.

senses.definition: A small genus of aromatic plants in the mint family, found in western North America.
examples: Audibertia species are common in dry Californian hills.
The botanist collected several samples of Audibertia.

senses.definition: Plural of audibility: the levels or degrees to which different sounds can be heard.
examples: The engineer measured the audibilities of several warning tones.
Different audibilities of the instruments affected the recording.

senses.definition: The quality of being loud or clear enough to be heard.
examples: Traffic noise reduced the audibility of the speakers.
The audibility of the alarm is checked every month.

senses.definition: To make something audible or easier to hear.
examples: The software audibilizes the signals from the device.
Good speakers can audibilize even a quiet voice.

senses.definition: Made audible or easier to hear.
examples: The low-frequency sounds were audibilized by special equipment.
Her whisper was audibilized through the microphone.

senses.definition: Makes something audible or easier to hear.
examples: The device audibilizes heartbeats for medical staff.
The system audibilizes data that would otherwise be silent.

senses.definition: Making something audible or easier to hear.
examples: The process of audibilizing the recordings took several hours.
Audibilizing these signals helps technicians find faults quickly.

senses.definition: Loud or clear enough to be heard.
examples: The teacher's voice was barely audible over the traffic.
There was an audible sigh of relief when the exam ended.

senses.definition: In American football, a spoken change of play called by the quarterback at the line of scrimmage.
examples: The quarterback called an audible when he saw the defence shift.
They practised different audibles for various situations.
collocations: change the play with an audible

senses.definition: In American football, changed the planned play by calling a new one aloud at the line of scrimmage.
examples: He audibled to a running play at the last second.
The coach was happy that the quarterback audibled when he saw the blitz.

senses.definition: The state of being able to be heard.
examples: The audibleness of the announcement is vital in an emergency.
Background music reduced the audibleness of the speaker's voice.

senses.definition: Plural of audibleness: different states or levels of being able to be heard.
examples: The recording compares the audiblenesses of several alarm tones.
Changing the room design altered the audiblenesses of the speakers.
